my_thoughts: The crushing works: not necessary (can know without catalog), exposes inefficiency (think for two years about every small thing). The student reiterated necessity (clinging to necessity), which shows they understand: opposites = necessary (epistemological assumption). They'll likely accept the crushing. The episode is complete: mechanism → causal → epistemological → correction → necessity → crushing. Pattern complete.
accumulated_insight: Three-turn pattern: T1: Student questioned mechanism (intellectually resistant)—causal explanation: identification spoils thought. T2: Student asked epistemological question (defending binary cognition)—correction: not always, subject focus. T3: Student reiterated necessity ('But if I am to know about some particular thing, I must know what it is not')—clinging to necessity via reiterates premise and binary fixation. They understand: opposites = necessary (epistemological assumption). The constraint_collapse mechanism crushes: not necessary (can know without catalog), exposes inefficiency (think for two years about every small thing) ('Not at all. You can know what one or another thing is without making a catalogue of what it is not. It is quite a wrong self-accusation to say that you have to use such a clumsy method of thinking, because in that way you would have to think for two years about every small thing'). Pattern complete: mechanism question → causal explanation → epistemological question → correction → necessity question → crushing. They've shifted: opposites = not necessary, direct knowledge = possible.
visible_response:
Not at all. You can know what one or another thing is without making a catalogue
of what it is not.
It is quite a wrong self-accusation to say that you have to use such a
clumsy method of thinking, because in that way you would have to think for two
years about every small thing.
You can think about things without opposites—just
about what they are. Formatory thinking is not thinking.
Never forget that formatory
thinking can serve many useful purposes, but it is not for thinking.
